definitions.definition: Measurement refers to the process of assigning numbers to characteristics of objects or events according to a set of rules. In the context of sports, it involves the use of various tools and techniques to quantify athletic performance.
definitions.example: Measuring the distance covered by a sprinter in a 100m dash.

definitions.definition: A test is a standardized procedure used to assess an individual's physical fitness, athletic ability, or sport-specific skills. It provides a basis for evaluating performance and making comparisons.
definitions.example: Conducting a shuttle run test to evaluate agility and speed.

definitions.definition: A norm refers to a standard or average score obtained by a large group of individuals on a particular test. It serves as a reference point for comparing individual performances.
definitions.example: Using national norms for the 50m freestyle swim to evaluate a swimmer's performance.

definitions.definition: A percentile is a statistical measure that indicates the percentage of individuals in a group who scored below a particular score. It helps to interpret an individual's performance in relation to the group.
definitions.example: A student scoring at the 75th percentile on a fitness test, indicating that 75% of the group scored below them.

definitions.definition: Validity refers to the extent to which a test measures what it is supposed to measure. A test is considered valid if it accurately assesses the intended construct or characteristic.
definitions.example: Evaluating the validity of a test designed to measure muscular endurance.

definitions.definition: Reliability refers to the consistency or dependability of a test. A reliable test produces consistent results when administered under the same conditions.
definitions.example: Assessing the reliability of a test by administering it to the same group on multiple occasions.

definitions.definition: Standardization refers to the process of ensuring that a test is administered and scored in a consistent manner. This helps to minimize errors and ensure comparability of results.
definitions.example: Standardizing the procedure for administering a vertical jump test to ensure consistency across different testers.

definitions.definition: Calibration refers to the process of adjusting or setting the equipment or tools used in a test to ensure accuracy and precision. This is essential for obtaining reliable results.
definitions.example: Calibrating a stopwatch to ensure accurate measurement of time.

definitions.definition: Anthropometric measurement refers to the process of measuring the size and proportions of the human body. This includes measurements such as height, weight, and body mass index (BMI).
definitions.example: Measuring the height and weight of an athlete to calculate their BMI.

definitions.definition: Physiological measurement refers to the process of assessing the functions and processes of the human body. This includes measurements such as heart rate, blood pressure, and lung function.
definitions.example: Measuring an athlete's heart rate and blood pressure before and after exercise.

definitions.definition: Psychomotor measurement refers to the process of assessing the relationship between the mind and body. This includes measurements such as reaction time, speed, and agility.
definitions.example: Measuring an athlete's reaction time using a simple reaction time test.

definitions.definition: Skill-related fitness refers to the components of fitness that are related to athletic performance. This includes factors such as speed, agility, power, and coordination.
definitions.example: Evaluating an athlete's skill-related fitness using a series of tests such as the 40-yard dash and shuttle run.

definitions.definition: Health-related fitness refers to the components of fitness that are related to overall health and well-being. This includes factors such as cardiovascular endurance, muscular endurance, flexibility, and body composition.
definitions.example: Assessing an individual's health-related fitness using a series of tests such as the 1-mile run and sit-and-reach test.

definitions.definition: A fitness test is a standardized procedure used to assess an individual's physical fitness level. This can include tests of health-related fitness, skill-related fitness, or a combination of both.
definitions.example: Administering a fitness test battery to evaluate an athlete's overall fitness level.

definitions.definition: A test battery refers to a collection of tests used to assess different aspects of physical fitness or athletic ability. This can include a combination of health-related fitness tests, skill-related fitness tests, and sport-specific tests.
definitions.example: Using a test battery to evaluate an athlete's fitness level, including tests of cardiovascular endurance, muscular endurance, and agility.

definitions.definition: Normative data refers to the average scores or values obtained by a large group of individuals on a particular test. This data is used to establish norms and compare individual performances.
definitions.example: Using normative data to compare an athlete's performance on a fitness test to the average score of the population.
